# Prologue {#prologue}

**What is Nighthawk?**  
Nighthawk is a Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS/HTTP2) performance characterization tool. Nighthawk is Envoy’s load generator and is written in C++. 

**Nighthawk and Meshery**  
Meshery integrates Nighthawk as one of (currently) three choices of load generator for characterizing and managing the performance of service meshes and their workloads. 

**Why GetNighthawk?**  
Nighthawk is growing in popularity, but the core project only builds to one architecture / one Docker image. Recently, Nighthawk is being improved so that it can be horizontally scalable \- such that multiple instances will be cognizant of one another and able to coordinate amongst each other. Nighthawk is a subproject of Envoy. Nighthawk is growing in popularity with Google, Red Hat, and AWS are investing into it. Istio is considering switching from Fortio to Nighthawk.

## Easing Management of the Nighthawk Lifecycle {#easing-management-of-the-nighthawk-lifecycle}

As with a lot of open source projects, there is a lack of consistent tooling. This makes it difficult to have easily repeatable tests in that the building, deploying, and maintaining of Nighthawk instances (potentially a fleet of Nighthawk instances) is a burden without additional tooling. The goal of the GetNighthawk project is to work with the Nighthawk community to address these lifecycle issues, facilitate Nighthawk adoption, and perhaps more importantly, enable a number of common user use cases through this tooling. This project aims to build tooling that provides general solutions to Nighthawk lifecycle problems that can be leveraged by the wider community. Within the CNCF Service Mesh Working Group and participants from the Layer5 community, we are working with end-users, vendors and cloud providers to do just that.

Commonly (and painfully) we repeatedly hear that it’s difficult to build Nighthawk. so this is the first issue we’ve tackled. With GetNighthawk, the Layer5 community, including key Nighthawk contributors and maintainers, users can leverage trusted, certified builds, distributed via the most popular package managers: apt, yum and Homebrew. For those using containers, we provide a distro-less Docker image. This means users don’t have to worry about having an entire OS worth of unnecessary packages lying around, reducing image size and the surface area for any malicious attackers.

## Enabling Standards-based, Distributed Performance Management {#enabling-standards-based,-distributed-performance-management}

Compatibility with the Service Mesh Performance (SMP) specification so that you can achieve consistency in your own performance management efforts and participate in the broader exchange of analysis and best practices of optimal operation of distributed systems, particularly in a Kubernetes environment and for workloads running on a service mesh.

# Project Purpose {#project-purpose}

GetNighthawk will provide generally-available distributions of Nighthawk under different architectures and platforms and easy-to-use tooling for installation and operation. This will include creating distributions of Nighthawk as well as augmenting existing tooling, Meshery, to retrieve these arch-specific packages and update their deployments.

## Goals {#goals}

1. **Further the state of distributed performance management.**  
   1. Enable standards-based, distributed performance management through compatibility with the [Service Mesh Performance](https://smp-spec.io) (SMP) specification.  
   2. Enable researchers and users ability for users to conveniently identify optimal service mesh configuration in consideration of their specific environment, applications and load  
      1. By orchestrating multiple instances of Nighthawk (see [horizontal scaling](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1UvCZwioQyqwb_RhQn14xAqZQ3yQX0P2BWFfHutimSmo/edit?usp=sharing))  
      2. By using adaptive load controller of Nighthawk

   

2. **Facilitate Nighthawk adoption.**   
   1. Deliver trusted, certified builds, distributed via the most popular package managers: apt, yum, Homebrew, and platforms: Docker and Meshery.  
   2. Bridge Nighthawk’s C++ with the lingua franca of Cloud Native: Golang.

3. **Deliver easy-to-use, repeatable tooling.**  
   1. To leverage Nighthawk as the performance characterization tool as used in the 30 patterns in the [Service Mesh Patterns](https://layer5.io/books/service-mesh-patterns) book.   
   2. Educate the ecosystem through the [CNCF Service Mesh Working Group](https://github.com/cncf/sig-network/tree/master/service-mesh-wg). 

# Objectives {#objectives}

What is both in and out of scope of the project.

## In Scope {#in-scope}

1. **Contribute stable and nightly builds for Debian, Ubuntu, CentOS and macOS.**  
   1. Distribution via apt, yum, Homebrew, Docker Hub.  
   2. Stable and nightly distro-less Docker image.  
   3. Add the Github Actions pipeline for it.  
2. A CLI for invoking and scheduling distributed performance tests using Meshery and Nighthawk. A command-line interface \- to fetch, run and debug Nighthawk using the mesheryctl cli.  
3. Nighthawk compatibility with the Service Mesh Performance (SMP) specification through Meshery’s ability to import and export performance test definitions in the SMP specification.  
4. Provide extensions loading for Nighthawk’s adaptive load component. Use go-nighthawk and mesheryctl as bridges to Golang-based applications.   
5. Provide the Nighthawk Docker image for each OS/architecture: rpm/deb/yum package

## Out of Scope {#out-of-scope}

1. Modifying nighthawk\_server, nighthawk\_client \- great tools. The GetNighthawk project will enhance and augment mesheryctl as the project’s CLI in order to deliver compatibility with SMP, orchestrate the deployment and teardown of distributed Nighthawk instances, schedule and characterize their tests, present and report results.

# Building Nighthawk {#building-nighthawk}

###  Current State {#current-state}

Nighthawk piggybacks on Envoy. A Docker image is produced and published to Docker Hub. Not a small build, but burns some CPU, takes some time to compile, which gives credence to the value of the GetNightawk project.

1. In terms of building, start with Nighthawk Readme, then move to Envoy’s Readme  
2. Do not need to build Envoy, however, the Nighthawk build leverages the same toolchain.  
3. In terms of a base image for Nighthawk, for a Debian-based distro,   
   1. … what is the oldest Ubuntu version that can be used to build Nighthawk (answering this assumes that later Ubuntu versions are compatible).  
4. The current Nighthawk build workflow is here \- [https://github.com/envoyproxy/nighthawk/blob/main/.circleci/config.yml](https://github.com/envoyproxy/nighthawk/blob/main/.circleci/config.yml) which requires the build image from Envoy.

### Artifacts naming conventions

Every artifact that is going to be published would follow the below naming convention:

1. The package is named with all small letters.  
2. Order of string prefixes:  
   1. Name of the binary (client/server/test\_server)  
   2. Name of the operating system (ubuntu/centos/darwin/windows)  
   3. The system architecture (amd64/32/arm)  
   4. Nighthawk version  
3. Proposed convention :  “\<2.a\>-\<2.b\>-2.c\>-2.d\>”

## Harnessing Nighthawk’s Horizontal Scalability {#harnessing-nighthawk’s-horizontal-scalability}

\[Otto\] High fidelity results generated local to a Nighthawk instance.  
Results to be centralized to a datasync.  
Aggregation of results to take place as well and be represented in histograms.  
A PR forthcoming from Otto on these capabilities. Likely to land in functional segments.

## Making sense of performance results {#making-sense-of-performance-results}

\[Sunku\] Characterizing performance when load is generated both internal and external to the cluster is valid. Different test cases; different analysis.

\[Otto\] Agrees that placing load generator outside of the cluster makes sense, however, it is interesting to note that many of the publicly published performance studies have configured their labs with cluster-internal load generation.

\[Lee\] [MeshMark](https://smp-spec.io) aims to offer a solution for simply and succinctly articulating the performance of a service mesh deployment (possibly a 0 to 100 scale). ([slide](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/14jUQgjVcm66B4fImdlqZllJicg2sYk0Q/edit#slide=id.ga49f2462fd_0_92))

\[Sunku\] Often need to deal with RFCs that characterize network performance at Layer 3, but these leave Layer 7 concerns unaddressed; e.g. 

## Adaptive Load Controller

Anirban Dasgupta  
Nisarg Joshi

# FAQs {#faqs}

## Can I still build Nighthawk myself? {#can-i-still-build-nighthawk-myself?}

Yes, certainly. As a matter of fact, you can leverage GetNighthawk’s build workflows as the basis for your own. We recommend collaborating in the GetNighthawk project to improve upon the workflows to meet your specific needs. [Join the community](http://slack.layer5.io) and let us know.

## Will nighthawk\_client and nighthawk\_server still be supported? {#will-nighthawk_client-and-nighthawk_server-still-be-supported?}

Yes, certainly. Their use cases are a subset of those within the Meshery project.

## What projects are related to GetNighthawk? {#what-projects-are-related-to-getnighthawk?}

Outside of the relationship between Meshery, Service Mesh Performance, and Nighthawk, the following projects are relevant:

* MeshMark (see [https://smp-spec.io](https://smp-spec.io))   
* Fortio ([http://fortio.org](http://fortio.org)), wrk2 ([https://github.com/giltene/wrk2](https://github.com/giltene/wrk2))   
* Go-Nighthawk ([https://github.com/layer5io/nighthawk-go](https://github.com/layer5io/nighthawk-go))

# Project  Site {#project-site}

The project site will be available at [https://getnighthawk.dev](https://getnighthawk.dev) (under construction now).

## Site Overview and Purpose {#site-overview-and-purpose}

Much of the GetNighthawk project’s value proposition will be able to be captured and described within a single page.

### Other Relevant Sites {#other-relevant-sites}

The following sites are similar in nature as to what getnighthawk.io will be:

* [https://smp-spec.io](https://smp-spec.io)  
* [https://www.getenvoy.io](https://www.getenvoy.io/)  
* [https://meshery.io](https://meshery.io)

## Site Scope and Structure {#site-scope-and-structure}

The following site sections have been accounted for in the site mockups.

* \[*your name here*\]Navbar  
* \[[Yash](https://github.com/Grumpyyash) \- [\#37](https://github.com/layer5io/getnighthawk/pull/37) \]Hero section  
  * Logo / Banner of GetNighthawk (required)  
  * GetNighthawk in action (?)  
* Or \`Get Started\` (?)  
* Current Release Version details / Project Status  
* Installation / setup / CDN resources / packages  
* \[ \]Why GetNighthawk /What is Nighthawk/Nighthawk and Meshery?  
* Video Section   
  * recorded meetings: [playlist](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL3A-A6hPO2INwi8A3NNClvdCxDoHa9NOU)  
  * recordings that demo the project:  
* Features  
  * Learn more about GetNighthawk\!   
  * Contributors (?) \~ Carousel \[collection\]  
  * Contrib Stats: Github (Forks, stars, watch)  
* Resources  
* \[ \]Performance Research Statistics   
* \[ \]Purpose of GetNightHawk  
* \[ \]FAQs  
* Example implementation  
* \[ \]Newsletter signup  
* \[ \]Footer section

      \= Visual Design Pending

*Possible future sections:*

* Blog \[collection\] (optional) \~ **DOCS**   
* Customer reviews

## Site Colors and Typography {#site-colors-and-typography}

Like any well-curated site, coherency of colors, purpose and use of colors and typography is considered for GetNighthawk. Given that GetNighthawk is largely stewarded by the Layer5 service mesh community and because the project intertwines with Meshery and SMP, the GetNighthawk project will be included in the family of Layer5 open source projects. As such, a common set of colors have been identified for use.

### Color Scheme {#color-scheme}

GetNighthawk will use the following color scheme:

/\* SCSS HEX \*/  
$charcoal: \#3c494fff;  
$keppel: \#00b39fff;  
$caribbean-green: \#00d3a9ff;  
$saffron: \#ebc017ff;  
$dark-jungle-green: \#1e2117ff;

*Figure \- GetNighthawk colors in SCSS*

![][image4]

*Figure \- GetNighthawk color scheme*

/\* SCSS Gradient \*/  
$gradient-top: linear-gradient(0deg, \#3c494fff, \#00b39fff, \#00d3a9ff, \#ebc017ff, \#1e2117ff);  
$gradient-right: linear-gradient(90deg, \#3c494fff, \#00b39fff, \#00d3a9ff, \#ebc017ff, \#1e2117ff);  
$gradient-bottom: linear-gradient(180deg, \#3c494fff, \#00b39fff, \#00d3a9ff, \#ebc017ff, \#1e2117ff);  
$gradient-left: linear-gradient(270deg, \#3c494fff, \#00b39fff, \#00d3a9ff, \#ebc017ff, \#1e2117ff);  
$gradient-top-right: linear-gradient(45deg, \#3c494fff, \#00b39fff, \#00d3a9ff, \#ebc017ff, \#1e2117ff);  
$gradient-bottom-right: linear-gradient(135deg, \#3c494fff, \#00b39fff, \#00d3a9ff, \#ebc017ff, \#1e2117ff);  
$gradient-top-left: linear-gradient(225deg, \#3c494fff, \#00b39fff, \#00d3a9ff, \#ebc017ff, \#1e2117ff);  
$gradient-bottom-left: linear-gradient(315deg, \#3c494fff, \#00b39fff, \#00d3a9ff, \#ebc017ff, \#1e2117ff);  
$gradient-radial: radial-gradient(\#3c494fff, \#00b39fff, \#00d3a9ff, \#ebc017ff, \#1e2117ff);

*Figure \- GetNighthawk color gradients in SCSS*

### Typography {#typography}

Qanelas Soft for headings and Open Sans for body text.

Repo: [layer5io/getnighthawk](https://github.com/layer5io/getnighthawk), publishing under \`/docs\` in master branch
